sections.label: Section 49
sections.translation: Leda , the daughter of Thestius , had three children , maidens ,

sections.label: Section 50
sections.translation: Phoebe , Clytemnestra my wife , and Helen ; the foremost of the favored sons of Hellas came to woo Helen ; but terrible threats of spilling his rival ’ s blood were uttered by each of them , if he should fail to win the girl .

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: τὸ πρᾶγμα δʼ ἀπόρως εἶχε Τυνδάρεῳ πατρί ,
sections.translation: Now the matter filled Tyndareus , her father , with perplexity , whether to give her or not , how he might best succeed . This thought occurred to him : the suitors should swear to each other and join right hands and pour libations

sections.label: Section 60
sections.translation: with burnt-sacrifice , binding themselves by this curse : whoever wins the child of Tyndareus for wife , they will assist that man , in case a rival takes her from his house and goes his way , robbing her husband of his rights ; and march against that man in armed array and raze his city to the ground ,

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: Ἕλληνʼ ὁμοίως βάρβαρόν θʼ ὅπλων μέτα .
sections.translation: Hellene no less than barbarian .

sections.label: Section 66
sections.original: ἐπεὶ δʼ ἐπιστώθησαν — εὖ δέ πως γέρων
sections.translation: Now when they had once pledged their word and old Tyndareus with no small cleverness had beguiled them by his shrewd device , he allowed his daughter to choose from among her suitors the one towards whom [*] the sweet breezes of Aphrodite might carry her .

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: ἣ δʼ εἵλεθʼ , ὅς σφε μήποτʼ ὤφελεν λαβεῖν ,
sections.translation: Her choice fell on Menelaus ; would she had never taken him ! Then there came to Lacedaemon from the Phrygians the man who , Argive legend says , judged the goddesses ’ dispute ; in robes of gorgeous hue , ablaze with gold , in true barbaric pomp ;

sections.label: Section 75
sections.translation: and he , finding Menelaus gone from home , carried Helen off , in mutual desire , to his steading on Ida . Goaded to frenzy , Menelaus flew through Hellas , invoking the ancient oath exacted by Tyndareus and declaring the duty of helping the injured husband .

sections.label: Section 80
sections.translation: And so the Hellenes , brandishing their spears and donning their harness , came here to the narrow straits of Aulis with armaments of ships and troops , with many horses and chariots , and they chose me to captain them all [*] for the sake of Menelaus ,

sections.label: Section 85
sections.translation: since I was his brother . Would that some other had gained that distinction instead of me ! But after the army was gathered and come together , we still remained at Aulis weatherbound . In our perplexity , we asked Calchas , the seer ,

sections.label: Section 90
sections.translation: and he answered that we should sacrifice my own child Iphigenia to Artemis , whose home is in this land , and we would sail and sack the Phrygians ’ capital if we sacrificed her , but if we did not , these things would not happen . [*] When I heard this , I commanded Talthybius

sections.label: Section 95
sections.translation: with loud proclamation to disband the whole army , as I could never bear to slay my daughter . Whereupon my brother , bringing every argument to bear , persuaded me at last to face the crime ; so I wrote in a folded scroll and sent to my wife ,

sections.label: Section 100
sections.translation: bidding her despatch our daughter to me on the pretence of wedding Achilles , at the same time magnifying his exalted rank and saying that he refused to sail with the Achaeans , unless a bride of our lineage should go to Phthia . Yes , this was the inducement I offered my wife ,

sections.label: Section 105
sections.translation: inventing , as I did , a sham marriage for the maiden . Of all the Achaeans we alone know the real truth , Calchas , Odysseus , Menelaus and myself ; but that which I then decided wrongly , I now rightly countermand again in this scroll , which you , old man , have found me

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: λύοντα καὶ συνδοῦντά μʼ εἰσεῖδες , γέρον .
sections.translation: opening and resealing beneath the shade of night . Up now and away with this missive to Argos , and I will tell you by word of mouth all that is written here , the contents of the folded scroll , for you are loyal to my wife and house .

sections.label: Section 1
sections.translation: Old man , come here and stand before my dwelling .

sections.label: Section 2
sections.translation: I come ; what new schemes now , king Agamemnon ?

sections.label: Section 3b
sections.translation: Will you hurry ?

sections.label: Section 3c
sections.translation: I am hurrying . It is little enough sleep old age allows me

sections.label: Section 5
sections.translation: and keenly it watches over my eyes .

sections.label: Section 6
sections.original: τίς ποτʼ ἄρʼ ἀστὴρ ὅδε πορθμεύει ;
sections.translation: What can that star be , steering its course there ?

sections.label: Section 7
sections.translation: Sirius , still shooting over the zenith on his way near the Pleiads ’ sevenfold track .

sections.label: Section 9
sections.translation: The birds are still at any rate

sections.label: Section 10
sections.translation: and the sea is calm , hushed are the winds , and silence broods over the Euripus .

sections.label: Section 12
sections.original: τί δὲ σὺ σκηνῆς ἐκτὸς ἀίσσεις ,
sections.translation: Then why are you outside your tent , why so restless , my lord Agamemnon ? All is yet quiet here in Aulis ,

sections.label: Section 15
sections.translation: the watch on the walls is not yet astir . Let us go in .

sections.label: Section 16b
sections.translation: I envy you , old man , yes , and every man who leads a life secure , unknown and unrenowned ; but little I envy those in office .

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: καὶ μὴν τὸ καλόν γʼ ἐνταῦθα βίου .
sections.translation: And yet it is there that we place the be-all and end-all of existence . [*]

sections.label: Section 21
sections.original: τοῦτο δέ γʼ ἐστὶν τὸ καλὸν σφαλερόν ,
sections.translation: Yes , but that is where the danger comes ; and ambition , [*] sweet though it seems , brings sorrow with its near approach . At one time the unsatisfied claims of the gods

sections.label: Section 25
sections.translation: upset our life , at another the numerous peevish fancies of our subjects shatter it .

sections.label: Section 28
sections.translation: I do not like these sentiments in one who is a chief . It was not to enjoy all blessings that

sections.label: Section 30
sections.translation: Atreus begot you , Agamemnon ; but you must experience joy and sorrow alike , mortal as you are . Even though you like it not , this is what the gods decree . But you , after letting your taper spread its light abroad ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.translation: write the letter which is still in your hands and then erase the same words again , sealing and reopening the tablet , then flinging it to the ground with floods

sections.label: Section 40
sections.translation: of tears , and leaving nothing undone in your aimless behavior to stamp you mad . What is it that troubles you ? what news is there affecting you , [*] king ? Come , share with me your story ;

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: πρὸς δʼ ἄνδρʼ ἀγαθὸν πιστόν τε φράσεις ·
sections.translation: you will be telling it to a loyal and trusty heart ; for Tyndareus sent me that day to form part of your wife ’ s dowry and to wait upon the bride with loyalty .

sections.label: Section 115
sections.translation: Daughter of Leda , in addition to my first letter , I am sending you word —.

sections.label: Section 117
sections.original: λέγε καὶ σήμαινʼ , ἵνα καὶ γλώσσῃ
sections.translation: Say on and make it plain , that what my tongue utters may accord with what you have written .

sections.label: Section 119
sections.original: μὴ στέλλειν τὰν σὰν ἶνιν πρὸς
sections.translation: Not to despatch your daughter to

sections.label: Section 120
sections.translation: Euboea ’ s deep-gulfed wing , to the waveless bay of Aulis , for after all we will celebrate our child ’ s wedding at another time .

sections.label: Section 124
sections.translation: And how will Achilles , cheated of his bride ,

sections.label: Section 125
sections.translation: curb the fury of his indignation against you and your wife ? Here also is a danger . [*] Make clear what you are saying .

sections.label: Section 128
sections.translation: It is his name , not himself that Achilles is lending , knowing nothing of the marriage or of my scheming

sections.label: Section 130
sections.translation: or my professed readiness to betroth my daughter to him for a husband ’ s embrace . [*]

sections.label: Section 133
sections.translation: A dreadful venture yours , king Agamemnon , you that , by promise of your daughter ’ s hand to the son of the goddess ,

sections.label: Section 135
sections.translation: were bringing the maid here to be sacrificed for the Danaids .

sections.label: Section 136
sections.translation: Ah me ! I am utterly distraught ; alas ! bewilderment comes over me . Away ! hurry your steps ,

sections.label: Section 140
sections.translation: yielding nothing to old age .

sections.label: Section 140b
sections.translation: I will make haste , king .

sections.label: Section 141
sections.translation: Do not sit down by woodland fountains ; scorn the witcheries of sleep .

sections.label: Section 143
sections.translation: Hush ! [*]

sections.label: Section 144
sections.translation: And when you pass any place where roads diverge ,

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: λεῦσσε , φυλάσσων μή τίς σε λάθῃ
sections.translation: cast your eyes all round , taking heed that no mule-wagon eacape you , passing by on rolling wheels , bearing my child to the ships of the Danaids .

sections.label: Section 149
sections.translation: It shall be so .

sections.label: Section 149b
sections.translation: Start then from the bolted gates , [*]

sections.label: Section 150
sections.translation: and if you meet the escort , start them back again , and drive at full speed to the abodes of the Cyclopes .

sections.label: Section 153
sections.original: πιστὸς δὲ φράσας τάδε πῶς ἔσομαι ,
sections.translation: But tell me , how shall my message find credit with your wife or child ?

sections.label: Section 155
sections.translation: Preserve the seal which you bear on this tablet . Away ! Already the dawn is growing grey , lighting the lamp of day and the fire of the sun ’ s four steeds ;

sections.label: Section 160
sections.translation: help me in my trouble . Exit Old man . No mortal is prosperous or happy to the last , for no one was ever born to a painless life . Exit Agamemnon .

sections.label: Section 1.164
sections.translation: To the sandy beach

sections.label: Section 1.165
sections.translation: of sea-coast Aulis I have come after a voyage through the tides of narrow Euripus , leaving Chalcis , my city which feeds the waters
